g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of registers in microcontrollers. Today we dive deep into the 8051 architecture's register organization.
Registers are small, fast storage locations within the CPU that hold data, addresses, and control information during execution.
g.text: • Small, fast storage locations within the CPU
• Hold data, addresses, and control information during program execution
• Fundamental building blocks of any microcontroller system

g.data-narration: Understanding registers is essential for embedded systems programming as all operations involve reading from or writing to registers.
g.text: • All microcontroller operations ultimately involve registers
• Foundation for assembly language programming and interrupt handling
• Enable efficient code and effective hardware debugging

g.data-narration: The 8051 has 32 general-purpose registers organized in four banks of 8 registers each, occupying internal RAM addresses 00H-1FH.
g.text: 32 registers (R0-R7) organized in four banks of 8 registers each
Each bank occupies 8 bytes of internal RAM
The active bank is selected using RS0 and RS1 bits in PSW register

g.data-narration: Register bank selection uses PSW bits RS1 and RS0. Four banks are selected by combinations of these two bits representing values 0-3.
g.text: Bank = (RS1 × 2) + RS0 — Select active bank using PSW register bits
Enables quick context switching in interrupt service routines without saving all registers to memory

g.data-narration: The 8051 provides 21 Special Function Registers controlling various operations. They are located in upper RAM at addresses 80H-FFH.
g.text: Located in upper 128 bytes of internal RAM (addresses 80H-FFH)
Include accumulator, B register, PSW, ports, timers, and serial communication

g.data-narration: The Accumulator (ACC at E0H) is the primary register for arithmetic and logical operations. The B register works with ACC for multiplication and division.
g.text: • Most instructions use ACC as operand
• MUL AB: A=low byte, B=high byte

g.data-narration: The Program Status Word (PSW) at address D0H contains 8 flags: CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, and P, indicating operation results.
The Data Pointer (DPTR) is a 16-bit register for external memory access and table lookups. The Program Counter (PC) points to the next instruction.
The Stack Pointer (SP) is an 8-bit register pointing to the top of the stack. After reset, SP is initialized to 07H, meaning stack starts at address 08H.
g.text: • Set SP to 60H+ for user applications

g.data-narration: The 8051 has four port registers (P0-P3) for I/O operations. The bit-addressable RAM region (20H-2FH) allows individual bit access.
This example demonstrates switching between register banks using the PSW register. Each bank is selected by different RS1 and RS0 bit combinations.
This example shows using DPTR with MOVC instruction for table lookup. The accumulator index retrieves corresponding values from program memory.
Understanding PSW flags is critical. CY indicates unsigned overflow while OV indicates signed overflow. This example shows flag behavior after addition.
Let us recap the key formulas: register bank selection uses RS1 and RS0 bits, DPTR forms 16-bit addresses, and stack grows upward with SP.
g.text: SP increments before PUSH, decrements after POP
Important Addresses: ACC=E0H | B=F0H | PSW=D0H | SP=81H | DPL=82H | DPH=83H | P0=80H | P1=90H | P2=A0H | P3=B0H

g.data-narration: Avoid common mistakes: remember stack grows upward, CY vs OV differ, and not all SFRs are bit-addressable. Focus on understanding flag behavior.
g.text: • Confusing register bank addresses (00H-1FH) with SFR addresses
• Memorize key SFR addresses | Practice flag analysis | Review PSW bit positions
After reset: SP=07H, PC=0000H, PSW=00H (Bank 0), Ports=FFH (inputs)
